metadata.title: US Cement Imports by Country of Origin
metadata.description: Detailed breakdown of US cement and clinker imports by source country, pricing, logistics, and trade policy exposure
metadata.notes: Import volumes include both finished cement and clinker. Clinker imports are ground domestically at receiving terminals.

import_origins.section_301_exposure: MEDIUM - Not currently covered but could be targeted in future trade actions
risk_factors: Potential antidumping risk if volumes surge
import_origins.trend: Largest and growing source. Turkish exporters benefit from currency depreciation and excess capacity.

import_origins.section_301_exposure: LOW - USMCA trade agreement provides duty-free access
risk_factors: Canadian carbon pricing increasing production costs
import_origins.trend: Stable, mature trade flow. Great Lakes seasonal pattern. St. Marys (Votorantim) is dominant US-bound exporter.

import_origins.section_301_exposure: LOW - EU/NATO ally, no active trade disputes
competitive_advantages: Titan Cement owns US terminals (vertically integrated)
risk_factors: EU CBAM potential impact on exports
import_origins.trend: Titan Cement dominant exporter with integrated US distribution (Roanoke Cement, terminals). Stable share.

import_origins.section_301_exposure: LOW - US strategic ally, bilateral trade cooperative
competitive_advantages: Very low production costs (subsidized energy historically)
Suez Canal proximity for Gulf/Atlantic routing
import_origins.trend: Growing source, especially to Houston via SESCO. Egyptian overcapacity (100+ mtpa capacity vs 50 mtpa domestic demand) drives aggressive export pricing.

import_origins.section_301_exposure: MEDIUM - Vietnam subject to increasing trade scrutiny; potential Section 301 targeting
import_origins.trend: Fastest-growing import source. Vietnamese overcapacity and ultra-low costs driving West Coast penetration.

import_origins.section_301_exposure: HIGH - China remains subject to broad Section 301 tariffs; cement could be targeted if volumes increase
import_origins.trend: Modest volumes, constrained by trade policy risk. Primarily clinker and white cement where domestic supply is limited.

import_origins.section_301_exposure: LOW - US-Colombia Trade Promotion Agreement in effect
competitive_advantages: Short transit times to Gulf/SE US
import_origins.trend: Stable flow, primarily Argos serving its own US distribution network post-Summit Materials acquisition.

competitive_advantages: Lower shipping costs than Mediterranean sources
import_origins.trend: Niche Florida-focused supply. Short transit advantage for Florida market.

import_origins.section_301_exposure: LOW - USMCA provides duty-free access. Historic AD order terminated 2006.
risk_factors: Mexico domestic demand growth reducing export surplus
import_origins.trend: Reduced from historic levels. Mexico AD order (1990-2006) created long trade disruption. Now primarily CEMEX/GCC supplying their own US operations.

import_origins.section_301_exposure: LOW - US-Japan alliance, no trade disputes on cement
risk_factors: Higher production costs than developing countries
import_origins.trend: Stable niche, primarily Taiheiyo supplying CalPortland. Premium quality positioning.
Small but growing West Coast supply source.

risk_factors: Panama Canal constraints for Gulf delivery
import_origins.trend: Emerging West Coast source. SCG is major regional exporter.

current_tariff_regime.notes: Portland cement (HTS 2523.29) enters US duty-free under MFN. No current tariffs on cement from any origin.

historical_cases.outcome: Terminated after sunset review. CEMEX was primary target.
historical_cases.significance: Demonstrated US industry willingness and ability to file AD cases on cement

section_232_exposure.status: Cement not currently subject to Section 232 investigation
section_232_exposure.risk: LOW - Cement is not classified as national security commodity
section_232_exposure.notes: Steel/aluminum Section 232 tariffs do not extend to cement

section_301_exposure.china_tariffs: Cement not specifically listed under current Section 301 China tariffs, but could be added in future lists
section_301_exposure.other_countries: No Section 301 actions pending against Turkey, Egypt, Vietnam for cement
section_301_exposure.risk_assessment: MEDIUM for China/Vietnam; LOW for other sources

buy_america.baba_status: Build America Buy America (BABA) Act applies to cement as construction material
buy_america.current_waiver: Nationwide waiver for cement and cementitious materials through 2026
buy_america.impact_if_enforced: Would exclude imported cement from federally funded projects (~15% of demand)
buy_america.industry_position: PCA supports BABA; importers seek waiver extension

carbon_border_adjustment.us_status: No US CBAM enacted as of 2026
carbon_border_adjustment.eu_cbam_impact: EU CBAM affects European cement costs, may make EU exports less competitive
carbon_border_adjustment.risk: If US enacts CBAM, imports from high-carbon producers (China, Vietnam, Egypt) would face surcharges
carbon_border_adjustment.estimated_impact_per_ton: $5-$25 depending on origin country carbon intensity
